summary:
The child presents with potential signs of Sydenham’s chorea without other classic rheumatic fever manifestations; hence, detecting a prior streptococcal infection through Antistreptolysin O titers remains critical as these infections precede development of rheumatic complications. This blood test identifies post-streptococcal immunity indicating likely causation by Group A Strep., supporting diagnosis when direct observations are lacking. 

Note how I condensed Agent2's detailed explanation into two concise yet comprehensive statements focusing strictly on clinically pertinent information related directly back towards addressing the core diagnostic query posed about identifying underlying cause via appropriate lab workup options available based upon presented symptomatology patterns noted initially described within context provided earlier herein above.
